attributes.stem: A 58-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for routine follow-up of vulval lichen sclerosus, which was diagnosed five years ago. She reports ongoing intermittent vulval itch despite regular use of topical corticosteroids. She has no other medical conditions and takes no other regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Vulval examination reveals pallid, atrophic changes with architectural distortion including labial fusion and narrowing of the introitus. There are no ulcers, masses, or suspicious lesions.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most significant long-term risk associated with this condition?
attributes.explanation:
Lichen sclerosus is a chronic inflammatory condition that primarily affects the anogenital region. The most significant long-term concern is malignant transformation. Vulval squamous cell carcinoma develops in approximately 3-5% of women with lichen sclerosus, necessitating regular monitoring and prompt investigation of any suspicious changes.

Urinary incontinence can occur due to architectural changes and scarring associated with lichen sclerosus, but it is not the most significant long-term risk. While it may affect quality of life, it does not pose the same level of health risk as malignant transformation.

Chronic pain syndrome can develop in some patients with lichen sclerosus, particularly if there is significant scarring or fissuring. However, this is generally manageable with appropriate treatment and is not life-threatening.

Complete labial fusion is a potential complication of untreated or poorly controlled lichen sclerosus. While this can cause significant symptoms and may require surgical correction, it is not as serious as the risk of malignancy.

Recurrent candidiasis is not typically associated with lichen sclerosus. While patients may experience secondary infections, these are usually manageable with appropriate treatment.

Regular follow-up is essential for patients with lichen sclerosus, typically every 6-12 months, to monitor for signs of malignant transformation. Patients should be educated about self-examination and the importance of reporting any changes such as persistent ulceration, nodules, or masses promptly. Adequate treatment with potent topical corticosteroids may help reduce the risk of malignant transformation.
learningPoints: Lichen sclerosus is associated with a 3-5% lifetime risk of developing vulval squamous cell carcinoma.
Regular monitoring of vulval lichen sclerosus is essential due to the risk of malignant transformation.
Patients with lichen sclerosus should be educated about self-examination and prompt reporting of changes.
references: Vieira-Baptista P, Pérez-López FR, López-Baena MT, Stockdale CK, Preti M, Bornstein J. Risk of Development of Vulvar Cancer in Women With Lichen Sclerosus or Lichen Planus: A Systematic Review. J Low Genit Tract Dis. 2022 Jul 1;26(3):250-257. doi: 10.1097/LGT.0000000000000673. Epub 2022 Mar 11. PMID: 35285455.
